topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in Maya’s inbox like a physical blow. A week. A complete shift from the meticulous world of data analysis to the chaotic, high-pressure environment of client relations. Her hands, usually steady when navigating complex spreadsheets, started to tremble slightly as she reread the transfer memo. The office hum seemed suddenly louder, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an aggressive intensity. She spent the rest of the afternoon staring blankly at her monitor, the words swimming before her eyes. When her manager, David, called her in, Maya found herself gripping the edge of the conference table so tightly her knuckles were white. "So, the marketing team," David began, his tone annoyingly cheerful, "we need you to bridge the gap." Maya managed a tight, strained smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Right," she murmured, the sound thin in the quiet room. She felt a distinct pressure building behind her sternum, a need to physically retreat, to find a corner where the sudden upheaval couldn't reach her.

[story 2]

Liam watched the organizational chart on the wall, the familiar names and departments suddenly seeming alien and distant. The notification about his reassignment to the product development unit had hit him mid-morning, and a strange, cold knot tightened in his stomach. He kept running a hand over his jaw, a nervous habit he hadn't indulged in since his first day. Every time a colleague walked past his desk, Liam felt an involuntary tightening in his chest, a sudden awareness of being adrift. "This is... a lot," he managed to say to his desk mate, his voice pitched a little too high. He avoided eye contact, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sun slanting through the window. The prospect of learning an entirely new set of skills made his breath catch, shallow and quick. He wanted nothing more than to disappear into the noise of the coffee machine.

[story 3]

Sarah reread the departmental restructuring announcement three times, her breath catching in short, shallow gasps. The move to international compliance felt less like a promotion and more like being dropped onto an uncharted island. She walked to the water cooler, needing the momentary distraction of the cool metal against her palms. When her supervisor, Mr. Henderson, approached, Sarah felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to shrink into her sensible cardigan. "I'm really looking forward to this new challenge," she articulated, the words sounding rehearsed and hollow. She focused intently on the pattern of the tiles on the floor, trying to anchor herself against the swirling uncertainty in her mind. A faint sheen of perspiration broke out on her forehead, a physical manifestation of the sheer disorientation washing over her.
